audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Two students meet on the university campus.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: They start a conversation together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For each of the questions, four alternatives are given.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Decide which of the alternatives a, b, c or d best fits what you hear on the tape.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And circle the appropriate letter.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to the example will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me, um, can you help me?
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I was looking for the main hall.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Maybe I can, actually.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I'm looking for the main hall too.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. I think it's in the administration building.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Are you a new student?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, I am.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: The man says he's looking for the main hall too, so a has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first conversation, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me, um, can you help me?
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I was looking for the main hall.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Maybe I can, actually.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I'm looking for the main hall too.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. I think it's in the administration building.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Are you a new student?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, I am.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Yeah, I thought you looked as lost as me.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: So, I'm trying to find the admin building too, so I can register for my course.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I don't seem to be having much luck.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Well, um, look, according to this map of the campus here, you go straight up the steps, uh huh, turn left.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: And the building on the right.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: OK, let's see if we can find it.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, this looks right.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, yeah, it must be.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Look, there are hundreds of other people here.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, there must be at least 50 people in the queue.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: We'll be here till gone 2:00 at this rate.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'm starving.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: So am I.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Actually, I was on my way to the canteen to get something for lunch.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. Why don't I go to the canteen and buy something, and you stay here and wait?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: What would you like?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Pizza, sandwich, hot dog, fried rice, they do everything.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, something easy.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Takeaway fried rice sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: OK, fried rice.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: No, no, on on second thoughts, I'll have a cheese and tomato sandwich.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Right, 1 cheese and tomato.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Anything to drink?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, get me a coffee, would you?
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Uh. Hot coffee is a bit hard to carry.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: What about a Coke or an orange juice?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Um. Get me an orange juice then.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Look, here's five dollars.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, take two dollars back, it shouldn't cost me more than three dollars.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Well, keep the five and we'll sort it out later.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, and could you give me an apple as well?
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: OK, back in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: The woman speaks to the clerk about registering at university.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen, complete the form by filling in the numbered spaces 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I'm here to register for the first year law course.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, I'll just have to fill out this form for our records.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Um. What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Julia Perkins.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Can you spell it for me?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's um, JULIA PERKINS.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Address?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Flat 5, 15 Waratah Road, that's WARATAH, Brisbane.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Brisbane.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, and your telephone number?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, we haven't got the phone on yet. We've only just moved in.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: OK, well can you let us have the number once the phone's connected, and I'll make a note here to be advised.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: And the course?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: I beg your pardon?
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: What course are you doing?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, um, First Year Law.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Well, you'll have to go across to the Law Faculty, and get this card stamped,
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: and then you come back here with it and pay your union fee.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: The man and the woman meet up again.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Look at questions 11 and 12, and circle the correct answer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Oh, there you are.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, thought you were never gonna come back.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Ha, ha, sorry.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: The canteen was absolutely packed, I had to wait for ages.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: And then when I got to the front of the queue, they had hardly any food left.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: So I had to get you a slice of pizza.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I'm Sorry.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I could eat anything.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I'm so hungry.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, and there's your bottle of orange juice.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: And your apple.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: At least I managed that.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Great, thanks a lot.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, and here's your $2 back.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Don't worry about it.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Buy me a cup of coffee later.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Yeah, all right then.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: So, how did you go?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, well, in order to register, we've got to go to the law faculty and get this card stamped.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: And then go back to the admin building and pay the union fees.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: That means we're registered.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: After that we have to go to the notice board to find out about lectures, and then we have to put our names down for tutorial groups, and go to the library.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Well, first, let's sit down and have our lunch.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What must the students do as part of registration at the university?
options: Check the notice board in the Law Faculty.

answer_key.correct_answer: to be advised | not connected | no phone | none | not given

audioscript.text: Section 2, you are going to hear a talk about banks in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 13 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the talk and complete the notes by filling in the number spaces 13 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. OK. Right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Thanks for turning up today, thanks for turning up today to this short talk, I'm going to give on student banking.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Many of you are unfamiliar with the way banks work in this country, and today's talk should just give you a few starting points.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. I will of course answer any questions at the end.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, well, as you probably know, you'll need to open a bank account while you're here, and it's the safest place to keep your money, and it's best to open an account with one of the major banks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You should each have a handout with the names and addresses...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's there's Barclays in Realty Square, National Westminster in Preston Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Lloyds in City Plaza and Midland in Hope Street.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, all these banks offer special student accounts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, it's important to note that as an international student, you'll not necessarily be eligible for all the facilities offered to resident students.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, as an international student, you will need to provide evidence that you can fund yourself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For however long your course lasts, um, banks have different policies, and the services that they'll offer you will depend on your individual circumstances, and on the discretion of the bank manager involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, um, it's a matter of going there and finding out about your own particular situation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Um. When you do go to open a bank account, you should take some documentation with you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. I've already mentioned that you must be able to support yourself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition to this, most banks ask you to bring your passport and your letter or certificate of enrollment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, now, by far the most useful type of account to open is a current account.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When you do this, you will actually get what is called a student account, which is a current account with special concessions for students.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When you open the account, the bank will give you a chequebook, and you can use this to draw money out as you need it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you need to write cheques in shops.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll also need a cheque card.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is really an identity card, which guarantees that correctly written cheques up to the value stated on the card will be honored by the bank.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 18 to 21.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the rest of the talk, complete the rest of the notes in the spaces numbered 18 to 21.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, uh, if you want to draw out cash for yourself, you can make the cheque payable in your own name or to cash.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can also withdraw cash from a cash point machine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With a cash card.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, these are extremely useful as they enable you to withdraw cash from your account during the day or at night.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Um. There is also another card called Switch or Delta, and you can use this to pay for things in shops.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Um. It takes the money right out of your account, so you don't need your chequebook.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, you may want to take more money out of the bank than you have in it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Huh, yeah, this is called having an overdraft.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Be very careful with this, you should not do this without permission from your bank.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Overdrafts usually incur charges, though some banks offer interest free overdrafts to some students, but find out before you get one, right?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, that just leaves opening times.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ha, when can you go?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Banks used to be open from 9:30 am until 3:30 pm from Monday to Friday.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But many main branches are now open until 4:30 or 5:00 pm on weekdays, and some of the bigger branches in London and other major cities are now open for a limited time on Saturdays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, um, any questions?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you are going to hear a talk about banks in the UK.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 13 to 17.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen to the talk and complete the notes by filling in the number spaces 13 to 17.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Uh. OK. Right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. Thanks for turning up today, thanks for turning up today to this short talk, I'm going to give on student banking.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. Many of you are unfamiliar with the way banks work in this country, and today's talk should just give you a few starting points.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. I will of course answer any questions at the end.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right, well, as you probably know, you'll need to open a bank account while you're here, and it's the safest place to keep your money, and it's best to open an account with one of the major banks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You should each have a handout with the names and addresses...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's there's Barclays in Realty Square, National Westminster in Preston Park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Lloyds in City Plaza and Midland in Hope Street.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, all these banks offer special student accounts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, it's important to note that as an international student, you'll not necessarily be eligible for all the facilities offered to resident students.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, as an international student, you will need to provide evidence that you can fund yourself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For however long your course lasts, um, banks have different policies, and the services that they'll offer you will depend on your individual circumstances, and on the discretion of the bank manager involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, um, it's a matter of going there and finding out about your own particular situation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Um. When you do go to open a bank account, you should take some documentation with you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. I've already mentioned that you must be able to support yourself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition to this, most banks ask you to bring your passport and your letter or certificate of enrollment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, now, by far the most useful type of account to open is a current account.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When you do this, you will actually get what is called a student account, which is a current account with special concessions for students.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When you open the account, the bank will give you a chequebook, and you can use this to draw money out as you need it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you need to write cheques in shops.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll also need a cheque card.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is really an identity card, which guarantees that correctly written cheques up to the value stated on the card will be honored by the bank.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now look at questions 18 to 21.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As you listen to the rest of the talk, complete the rest of the notes in the spaces numbered 18 to 21.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Right, uh, if you want to draw out cash for yourself, you can make the cheque payable in your own name or to cash.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can also withdraw cash from a cash point machine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With a cash card.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, these are extremely useful as they enable you to withdraw cash from your account during the day or at night.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Um. There is also another card called Switch or Delta, and you can use this to pay for things in shops.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Um. It takes the money right out of your account, so you don't need your chequebook.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, you may want to take more money out of the bank than you have in it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Huh, yeah, this is called having an overdraft.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Be very careful with this, you should not do this without permission from your bank.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Overdrafts usually incur charges, though some banks offer interest free overdrafts to some students, but find out before you get one, right?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, that just leaves opening times.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ha, when can you go?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Banks used to be open from 9:30 am until 3:30 pm from Monday to Friday.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But many main branches are now open until 4:30 or 5:00 pm on weekdays, and some of the bigger branches in London and other major cities are now open for a limited time on Saturdays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, um, any questions?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Note: May not be allowed all facilities given to resident students.
Must provide <input> I can support myself.
Services will depend on personal circumstances and discretion of Bank Manager.
items.title: Funding

items: Take with me: <input> and letter of enrolment.
Bank supplies: <input> and chequecard which guarantees cheques.
items.title: Opening an account

items: Cashcard: (you can <input> cash at any time.)
Switch/Delta cards: (take the money <input> the account.)
items.title: Other services

items: Most banks open until <input> during the week.
Some open for a limited time on Saturdays.
items.title: Opening times

body.title: STUDENT BANKING

answer_key.correct_answer: current | student | current account | student account
withdraw | draw | draw out | take out
permission of bank | permission from bank | permission of the bank | permission from the bank | bank permission | the bank permission
4.30 or 5 pm | 4.30 pm or 5 pm | 4:30 or 5 pm | 4:30 pm or 5 pm

test_text.title: The Aluminium Coke Can: A Presentation Idea

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Two students, Dawn and Ilmar, are discussing a project that they are working on together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 22 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the first part of the conversation, and complete the fact sheet by filling in the numbered spaces 22 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Dawn.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Ilmar.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: I'm glad I bumped into you.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: I've just found a great idea for the presentation we've got to do for Dr Banks next month.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: What, the one on everyday objects?
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Yes...
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Look at this article.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: It's really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Uh. The aluminum Coke can.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: You know, uh, Coca Cola can, soft drink cans.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Look, let's sit down here.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Have you got a minute?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Uh. Sure, I'll just get my bag.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: OK, so you think we can get a presentation out of this article?
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: I'm sure we can.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: First of all, we can provide some interesting facts about the aluminum cans that we drink out of every day.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Like?
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Well, here it says that in the Us, they produce 300 million aluminum drink cans each day.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Wow, three hundred million.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: That's an enormous number.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: It says here outstrips the production of nails or paper clips.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: And they say that the manufacturers of these cans exercise as much attention and precision in producing them as aircraft manufacturers do when they make the wing of an aircraft.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Let's have a look.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: They're trying to produce the perfect can, as thin but as strong as possible.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Mmm...
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: this bit's interesting...
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: "today's can weighs about 0.48 (nought point four eight) ounces: thinner than two pieces of paper..." from this magazine says.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Yeah, and yet it can take a lot of weight.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: More than 90 pounds of pressure per square inch, three times the pressure of a car tyre.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: OK, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: It's a good topic.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Now look at the diagram of the aluminum can, and at questions 26 to 31.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As the conversation continues, label the can by completing the notes in the numbered spaces 26 to 31.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What I thought was that we could do a large picture of a Coke can, and label it, and then talk about the different parts.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Look, I've done a rough picture here.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: OK, so where should we start?
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Well, the lid is complicated.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Let's start with the body first.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: I'll do a line from the center of the can.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Like this, and label it "body".
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: What does it say?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: It's made of aluminum, of course, and it's thicker at the bottom.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: So that it can take all that pressure.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: And then I think you should draw another line from the body for the label.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Label.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: The aluminum is ironed out until it's so thin that it produces what does it say?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Uh. A reflective surface suitable for decoration.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Apparently it helps advertisers too.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Yes, because it's so attractively decorated.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: And then there's the base.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Yes, it says the bottom of the can is shaped like a dome, so that it can resist the internal pressure.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: That's interesting.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: I didn't know that.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Nor did I.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: OK, so going up to the lid, there are several things we can label here.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: There's the rim around the edge which seals the can.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Got that.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: And there's a funny word for the seal, isn't there?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Yes, it's a flange.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: What does it say about it?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Well, the can's filled with Coke or whatever.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: And after that, the top of the can is trimmed, and then bent over to secure the lid.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: It looks like a seam.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: We could even do a blowup of it like this.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: FLANGE.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be clearer.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: I think we should label the lid itself.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: And say that it constitutes 25% of the total weight.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: 25 percent.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: So it's stronger than the body of the can.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Hmm. So to save money, manufacturers make it smaller than the rest of the can.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Didn't know that either.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: So, how do we open a can of coke?
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: First of all, there's the tab, which we pull up to open the can.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: And that's held in place by a rivet.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Hmm, I think that's too small for us to include.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: I agree, but we can talk about it in the presentation.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: We can show the opening though.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: That's the bit of the can that drops down into the drink when we pull the tab.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Yeah, hopefully.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: Sometimes the tab just breaks off.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Anyway, the opening is scored, so that it pushes in easily, but doesn't detach itself.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: OK, we can show that by drawing a shadow of it inside the can, like this, I'll label it scored opening.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Great, well, I think we've got the basis of a really interesting presentation.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Let's go and photocopy the article.
audioscript.speaker: Ilmar

audioscript.text: Fine, I'll take it home and study it some more.
audioscript.speaker: Dawn

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: An Introduction to Sports Studies at the University

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You are going to hear part of a short university lecture.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, look at questions 32 to 42.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and complete the lecture notes in the spaces numbered 32 to 42.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. Good morning and welcome to the University's open day, and to our mini lecture from the Sports Studies department.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Now, the purpose of this lecture is two fold.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: One, we want you to experience a university lecture to give you a taste of what listening to a university lecture is like.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: And two, we want you to find out something about the sports Studies program at this university.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So feel free to ask any questions during the talk, and I'll do my best to answer them.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: All right, so what does a course in sports studies involve?
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Well, you wouldn't be blamed for not knowing the answer to this question, because sports studies as a discipline is still comparatively new.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: But it's a growing area, and one which is now firmly established at our university.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Now, there are three distinct strands to sport studies, and you'd need to choose fairly early on just which direction you wanted to follow, and I'll just run over these now.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Firstly, we've got the sports psychology strand, secondly we've got the sports management strand, and last but not least, there's the sports physiology strand.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So just to recap, there's sports psychology, sports management, and sports physiology.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Uh. Let's look first at psychology.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Now the people who study Sports Psych want to work with top athletes, and they're looking at what will take those athletes that 1% extra.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: What makes them win, when all other things are equal, physically all other things are equal, they want to know what are the mental factors involved.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: The sports psychologist works closely with the athlete through his or her training program, and becomes an integral part of the team.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: In fact you could say that they play just as important a role as the coach.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So if you're interested in what makes people win, this could be the area for you.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Now secondly, we've got the strand which I refer to as sports management, and this goes hand in hand with the area of sports marketing, so you might like to think of this area as having two branches.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Management and marketing.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: On the management side, we look at issues relating to the running of sports clubs, management of athletes that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: But then on the other side, we've got sports marketing.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: And this is the side that interests me more, because here we will look at the market forces behind sport, questions like why do people spend their money on a football match or a tennis game rather than say on buying a CD or going to the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: What are those market forces?
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Sport used to just compete with sport.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Nowadays, it competes with other leisure activities.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: The spectators go to sport to be entertained rather than out of loyalty to a team.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: They want to have an evening out, and they don't want the cheap seats anymore.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: They want good seats, they want entertainment.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: And the professional sportsmen and women respond to this without question.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: They are there to give a performance, they provide the entertainment.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So in the marketing course, we address all these commercial issues, and we look at how this hooks back into the management of sport.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Now the third branch of Sport Studies sometimes comes under another name, and is also known as exercise science.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: And again here, we find that there are two distinct types of exercise science.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: The first is working very much at the macro level, what I call the huffing and puffing people.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So this looks at fitness testing, body measurements, all that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: But the more interesting side of sports physiology, at least in my view, is the side that looks at the micro level, looking at cellular change.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: They're doing cellular research, looking at changes in body cells when the body is under stress.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: So that just about brings us to the end of our mini lecture for today.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: I hope you found it interesting, I look forward to seeing you all on our course next year.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: But feel free to come and talk to me if you want any more information.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Um. I'll be over at that notice board near the main entrance.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: Thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: Lecturer

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER
